title: Top Five Vampire Movies and ‘Morbius’
format: Sean Fennessey and Van Lathan review 'Morbius,' then run down their favorite vampire movies. Later, Sean talks to Charlie McDowell about his one-location noir 'Windfall.'

films.title: Morbius
films.note: Sean thought it was flat-out bad: thin characters, laughable effects, a hard-R comic book movie without wit or sensuality, built around post-credits stingers that barely connect to the film itself. Van walked in with rock-bottom expectations and came out defending it. He liked the body-horror scene of Morbius reverting to his sickly human form, and says the disease-and-cure angle taps something vampire movies rarely explore.

segments.heading: Talking Vampires
segments.note: favorites and formative viewing, not a strict ranking

films.title: Blacula
films.note: Van's first vampire movie, tied to a family story about his grandmother knowing star Calvin Lockhart.

films.title: Salem's Lot
films.note: Terrified Van as a kid. Sean calls it one of the great vampire performances in movie history, directed by Tobe Hooper, even though it's technically a miniseries.

films.title: The Monster Squad
films.note: Part of Van's childhood vampire-movie pipeline.

films.title: Interview with the Vampire
films.note: Sean's touchstone for the Morbius comparison: Brad Pitt's cursed, self-aware vampire. Van respects it but prefers stories that pit vampires against humans rather than dwelling entirely inside vampire interiority.

films.title: Bram Stoker's Dracula
films.note: Francis Ford Coppola's "heady, amazing" entry, per Van.

films.title: Vampire's Kiss
films.note: Nicolas Cage going "so over the top, it's crazy."

films.title: Once Bitten
films.note: Jim Carrey's early vampire comedy, part of Van's backdrop of basic vampire movies.

films.title: Dracula
films.note: Sean's shoutout to the Tod Browning/Bela Lugosi original, one of the movies that hooked him on film as a kid.

films.title: From Dusk Till Dawn
films.note: One of Sean's favorites: a great action movie and a great vampire movie at once.

films.title: Nosferatu
films.note: Van calls it one of the most important movies ever made. Sean notes it's the film's 100th anniversary, with new Dracula/Nosferatu films reportedly coming from Karyn Kusama and Robert Eggers.

films.title: Vampires
films.note: John Carpenter's, and Van's actual favorite: humans hunting vampires, both fearless, James Woods and Daniel Baldwin, with Thomas Ian Griffith (Karate Kid Part III's Terry Silver) as the vampire lead.

films.title: I Am Legend
films.note: Van says the Will Smith version tilts more zombie than vampire.

films.title: The Omega Man
films.note: The earlier Charlton Heston take on the same story, closer to actual vampires per Van.

films.title: The Addiction
films.note: Abel Ferrara's black-and-white Lily Taylor movie, which Sean just rewatched for the first time in 20 years: a Cypress Hill and Onyx soundtrack, Fredro Starr in the cast.

films.title: The Lost Boys
films.note: Sean's pick, "a perfect 80s film," and Joel Schumacher's vampire movie.

films.title: Vampire in Brooklyn
films.note: Eddie Murphy playing it straight as a vampire didn't land for audiences at the time, per Van, but he says it holds up as a vampire film.

films.title: Def by Temptation
films.note: Van's honorable mention, a low-budget horror movie written and directed by its star James Bond III, shot by Ernest Dickerson, with Samuel L. Jackson. Set during the AIDS crisis; scared Van as a kid and still does.

films.title: Near Dark
films.note: Sean's number one: Kathryn Bigelow, Adrian Pasdar, Lance Henriksen, and Bill Paxton, with a Tangerine Dream score and a bar-massacre scene Sean still calls messed up.

films.title: Windfall
films.note: His Netflix noir, shot in one location with Jesse Plemons, Lily Collins, and Jason Segel, all three cast against type, including Segel as a genuine asshole.

films.title: The One I Love
films.note: His low-budget debut, one location and two actors, which he rewatched while prepping Windfall's blocking.

films.title: Force Majeure
films.note: Rewatched for how a single inciting incident ripples outward through a relationship.

films.title: Knife in the Water
films.note: Polanski's three-hander he studied for blocking a triangle of two men and a woman in one space.

films.title: Mank
films.note: Lily Collins was shooting this during their relationship. Watching the finished film, he was struck by how much she'd been carrying that he hadn't seen at home.

films.title: The Worst Person in the World
films.note: His pick for the last great thing he's seen.

films.title: The Power of the Dog
films.note: Praised for friends Kirsten Dunst and Jesse Plemons, who he calls two of the greatest actors in Hollywood.

films.title: The Hand of God
films.note: Paolo Sorrentino's film, "weird and funny," mood-dependent.

films: The Unbearable Weight of Massive Talent

The Worst Person in the World.title: The Worst Person in the World

The Unbearable Weight of Massive Talent.title: The Unbearable Weight of Massive Talent
